site.title: Bryon's OS
site.tagline: A living dashboard that turns chaos into motion.
site.description: A public operating board for Bryon's real work, real life, and real next moves — built to make priorities visible, decisions lighter, and momentum easier to keep.

Make the hero SKU, offer, channel, and KPI visible in one place
Turn merch pushes into repeatable attacks instead of random swings

Sales system, offer calendar, scorecard, and experiment log are built and ready to use
The structure is simple enough to run daily without turning into admin theater

current.content:
This is the merch attack board.

The point is not to admire the system.

The point is to know what is being sold, why it should move today, where it is being pushed, and what number proves the push worked.

data.title: Sick Sales War Room
data.summary: Reconcile the strategic revenue read with the daily merch operating layer so Sick can push the right offers, in the right lanes, with visible next moves.
data.nextAction: Turn the current read into a live board with revenue lanes, one hero merch push, one add-on, one channel plan, and one KPI target for today.

projects.content:
## Why it exists

Money is the job.

The first war room read made the business picture clearer: Sick is strongest when it organizes around event launches, subscription growth, impulse add-ons, hero merch SKUs, and cleaner offer structure.

The operating layer exists to make that insight usable every day instead of leaving it as a smart one-time analysis.

This page is the bridge between:

- the strategic revenue picture
- the current merch and inventory reality
- the next concrete move the team should make today

## Revenue engine

Current read from the earlier analysis:

- 90-day total sales around $903.4k
- biggest drivers were Online Store, POS, and subscriptions
- translation: the business is powered by online sales, event/POS energy, and recurring subscription revenue

## What is actually winning

The strategic pass says the business does best when it leans into:

- ticket and event energy
- magazine subscriptions and recurring revenue
- stickers, hats, and other easy add-on items
- a short list of hero merch SKUs instead of the whole catalog fighting for attention
- clean offer structure instead of fuzzy promotion

## Current hot zones

The earlier read flagged these as the current attention zones:

- Speedfest
- Sick Summer 2026
- Syclone giveaway
- Magazine subscriptions

These should shape campaign weight before random catalog pushes do.

## Current merch attack model

For day-to-day merch execution:

- pick one hero product, not ten equal products
- pair it with one sticker or add-on bundle to lift AOV
- drive one clear angle across site, email, and social the same day
- update a tiny scorecard daily so momentum is visible
- rotate creative based on hooks, not random guessing

## Board lanes

Use the war room in four operating lanes:

### Lane 1: Revenue now

- ticket launches
- Speedfest
- Sick Summer 2026
- any active premium offer that deserves campaign-level attention

### Lane 2: Retention / recurring

- quarterly subscriptions
- annual subscriptions
- upsells that turn one-time buyers into repeat revenue

### Lane 3: Merch winners

- hats
- stickers
- keychains
- Sick Spit
- giveaway merch
- any current hero SKU with enough stock and enough proof to push

### Lane 4: Cleanup

- reporting fixes
- discount / code attribution
- SKU rationalization
- separating evergreen merch from event merch and promo offers

## Current merch read from the operating layer

The structured triage pass against the current workspace export produced:

- actionable merch rows: 64
- Push What Is Left: 3
- Bundle / Test: 19
- Protect / Low Stock: 42
- Out of Stock Winners: 2067
- Dead / Review: 0

That says two things clearly:

- the catalog has a huge out-of-stock winner problem
- daily push decisions should focus on the very small pool that is both proven and actually available

## Current best merch push

Based on the current export:

- Hero: Sick The Mag Straw Hat
- Add-on: Sick The Mag Die Cut 5x5 Stickers
- Offer: Straw Hat + sticker
- Channel focus: Homepage + email + IG story

## Today board shape

The dashboard now treats the war room like a live operating board, not just a report.

It should answer five things fast:

- Now
- Next
- Watch
- Problems
- Moves

That gives the project a scan-first layer Bryon can use immediately without rereading all the reasoning every time.

## Main war room read

The business is not strongest when every product gets equal emotional importance.

It is strongest when the board organizes around:

1. event launches
2. subscription growth
3. impulse add-on merch
4. hero merch SKUs
5. clean offer structure

## Risks and weak spots

- discount attribution is still weak from the current export
- catalog noise is likely too high
- long-tail merch can steal attention from better offers
- evergreen merch, event merch, and promo / lead-gen offers need cleaner separation
- low-stock and out-of-stock winners can distort what looks promotable if the board is not kept current

## Non-negotiables

- one main push at a time
- every campaign needs a hook
- every hook needs a KPI
- do not let the whole catalog compete equally
- if a product is not moving, change the angle, image, placement, or offer quickly
- log experiments so the same bad idea does not get repeated as a new idea

## Morning questions

- What are we selling today?
- Why should someone care today?
- Is this a revenue-now push, a retention push, a merch-winner push, or a cleanup move?
- Where will they see it first?
- What add-on increases cart value?
- What number tells us by tonight if it worked?

data.title: SOABOS Dashboard
data.summary: Build a public home base that makes active work, real priorities, and the next move easier to see.
data.nextAction: Keep folding real operating systems into the board while making the design feel cleaner and easier to scan.

projects.content:
## Why it exists

Bryon needs one clean place to see what is in motion across life, work, and active builds without hunting through scattered apps and half-finished notes.

## Current direction

- content-first architecture
- public-safe by default
- conversation-driven updates
- publish fast without hand-rebuilding the site every time

data.title: Protect momentum across work and life
data.summary: Build a practical operating rhythm that supports work, family, and personal systems without making everything feel heavy.

goals.content: Momentum compounds when the next move is visible and the system is light enough to keep using.

data.title: Publish updates without cleanup headaches
data.summary: Make the dashboard reliable to ship so content changes can go live without broken paths, stray build artifacts, or manual patchwork.

goals.content: A system people trust gets updated. A system that feels fragile gets avoided.

data.title: Replace placeholders with real operating details
data.summary: Fill the dashboard with real projects, real focus, and useful descriptions so it starts feeling like a true working board.

goals.content: The dashboard should reflect reality early, even if it is still rough around the edges.

data.title: Reduce drift between ideas and execution
data.summary: Create enough structure that important work does not disappear between conversations, context switches, or busy days.

goals.content: The target is not maximum organization. It is keeping good work from slipping through the cracks.

data.title: Make priorities visible enough to act on
data.summary: Build a dashboard that keeps active projects, next actions, and useful progress visible without turning into clutter.

goals.content: The win is not more documentation. The win is less drift, less hunting, and faster motion.

areas.content: This area covers the systems that support the household: calendars, kid coordination, groceries, planning, and keeping the basics handled.

data.title: Personal
data.summary: The routines and systems that help life feel steadier, more intentional, and less reactive.

areas.content: This area is where reflection, habits, and quality-of-life support systems live.

data.title: Systems
data.summary: The dashboards, workflows, and operating structure that keep chaos from swallowing the important stuff.

areas.content: This area holds the backbone work: dashboards, routing, process design, and the tools that make the rest of the machine easier to run.

data.title: Work
data.summary: Client work, creative experiments, and business-facing projects that need to ship, perform, or stay organized.

areas.content: This area covers the projects tied to Bryon's day-to-day output, revenue, and brand-building work.

updates.content:
Created the initial repo structure for a public dashboard that can track goals, projects, areas, and current focus.

Established a content-first model so future updates can be made by editing structured files rather than manually changing the website for every small change.

data.title: Added sharper operating language and next-move structure

updates.content: Started shaping the dashboard around a more usable rhythm: what matters now, what happens next, what is in the way, and what already moved forward.

data.title: Publish flow cleanup and first real content pass

updates.content: Started replacing scaffold copy with real operating details, added more actual projects and goals, and tightened the publish path so future updates can go out with less cleanup.

data.title: Expanded dashboard into a real operating board

updates.content: Added more actual operating areas, seeded real project tracks across work, events, home, and systems, and moved the dashboard closer to a usable public view instead of a generic scaffold.

data.title: Added birthday tracking lane and refreshed the dashboard feel

updates.content: Pulled the birthday system into the dashboard, refreshed the current focus around real motion, and tightened the presentation so the board feels cleaner, newer, and more ready for ongoing updates.

data.title: Reframed the dashboard as a live command deck

updates.content:
Shifted the dashboard away from generic project-tracker language and closer to Bryon's actual operating style: cleaner decisions, clearer motion, and one visual board that can steer life, work, and active builds without feeling bloated.

The focus now is not just listing projects. It is helping the next move stand out fast.

data.title: Built phase 2 into a stronger command center

updates.content:
Added a more decisive homepage structure with ranked priority stacking, clearer operating lanes, a tighter updates feed, and a stronger mobile-first scan path.

The board now behaves more like a live command center and less like a flat collection of cards.

data.title: Added phase 3 gratitude lane to the dashboard

updates.content:
Built a dedicated daily gratitude widget into the command deck so today's prompt can live on the site as well as in Telegram.

The board now shows the active prompt, Entry ID, pending/completed state, and a short recent-history trail so the gratitude system is easier to use at night without scrolling back through chat.

data.title: Added phase 4 event and workout lanes

updates.content:
Extended the command deck with two more real-use lanes: an event-prep view for DJ work and a workout lane for training momentum.

Both lanes now follow the same content-first pattern as the gratitude lane, so the homepage can show upcoming event pressure, next prep moves, workout consistency, and the next session without hardcoding the UI each time.

data.title: Added the scorecard, offer calendar, and experiment log

updates.content:
Built the operating files behind the sales push so the team can run a repeatable daily cadence instead of guessing.

That includes a scorecard template, an experiment log, an offer calendar, and a direct playbook for how to attack shirts and stickers.

data.title: Reconciled the Sick sales war room strategy with the operating layer

updates.content:
Reconciled the original Sick War Room strategy read with the newer dashboard operating layer so the project now reflects both the business-level revenue view and the day-to-day merch decision system.

The strategic side says Sick is strongest when it organizes around:
- event launches
- subscription growth
- impulse add-on merch
- hero merch SKUs
- clean offer structure

The operational side now turns that into a visible daily board with:
- revenue-now lane
- retention / recurring lane
- merch-winner lane
- cleanup lane
- a current hero offer recommendation
- a simple scorecard / experiment rhythm

The current merch read from the workspace export now points to a tighter reality:
- actionable merch rows: 64
- Push What Is Left: 3
- Bundle / Test: 19
- Protect / Low Stock: 42
- Out of Stock Winners: 2067
- Dead / Review: 0

Current recommended merch push:
- Hero: Sick The Mag Straw Hat
- Add-on: Sick The Mag Die Cut 5x5 Stickers
- Offer: Straw Hat + sticker
- Channel focus: Homepage + email + IG story

Bottom line: the war room is no longer just a smart analysis or just a merch worksheet. It is now framed as a working board that connects revenue priorities, inventory reality, and daily action.

Follow-up pass: added a scan-first today board shape to wire directly into the dashboard detail view:
- Now
- Next
- Watch
- Problems
- Moves

Next pass: added execution assets directly into the war room detail view so the board now carries ready-to-use campaign pieces too:
- homepage slot copy
- email angle
- IG story frame sequence
- KPI target line

today.prompt: What is one part of your normal day that feels small on the surface but actually holds a lot of your peace together — and why are you more grateful for it than you used to be?
today.note: Open it in the morning from Telegram or come back to it at night from the dashboard without digging through chat.

history.note: Open it in the morning from Telegram or come back to it at night from the dashboard without digging through chat.

history.prompt: What is one object, spot, or creature in your day-to-day world that quietly makes life better every time you notice it — and what would feel missing if it were suddenly gone?
history.note: Open it in the morning from Telegram or come back to it at night from the dashboard without digging through chat.

history.prompt: What is something in your life right now that would have felt impossible, out of reach, or unreal to an older version of you — and why does having it now matter more than you usually admit?

history.prompt: What’s one person, place, or thing that made you feel quietly supported this week—and what about it hit deeper than usual?

now: Pick one hero offer and one channel stack for today.
Frame the day inside the revenue-now lane first, not across the full catalog.
Use one KPI target tonight: hero units, sticker attach rate, or bundle orders.
next: Press subscription upsells harder as the retention / recurring lane.
Turn Speedfest, Sick Summer 2026, and Syclone into premium board-level campaigns instead of general merch noise.
Build a short evergreen hero list so winning SKUs stay visible and reusable.
watch: Protect / Low Stock is 0, so good sellers can burn out fast if promoted blindly.
Out of Stock Winners is 0, which means restock gaps are distorting what looks sellable.
Tickets, subscriptions, stickers, hats, keychains, and giveaway merch still deserve heavier attention than random long-tail items.
problems: Discount and code attribution is still weak from the current export.
Catalog noise is too high, which makes decision weight too spread out.
Evergreen merch, event merch, and promo / lead-gen offers need cleaner separation.
moves: Homepage: feature the clearest hero SKU.
Email: lead with one hook, not a mixed catalog blast.
IG story: mirror the same offer stack as the homepage.
Cleanup lane: separate restock winners, protected low-stock items, and true push candidates.

homepage.subhead: Pair the hero with one easy add-on and one clear reason to buy today.

email.angle: Lead with one hero offer and keep the rest of the catalog quiet.
email.body: Today's move is simple: one hero product, one add-on, one reason to buy now.

igStory: Frame 1: one clean hero product for today.
Frame 2: Add one easy low-friction extra.
Frame 3: Show the offer stack clearly.
